summary: The hip bone is formed by the fusion of the ilium, ischium, and pubis, which all meet at the acetabulum.
The hip bone is formed by the fusion of only two bones: the ilium and the ischium.
The hip bone is a single bone that does not fuse from separate parts.
The femur is the longest and strongest bone in the body, transmitting body weight from hip to knee.
The tibia is the longest and strongest bone in the body.
The fibula is the main weight-bearing bone of the lower limb.
Intracapsular fractures of the femoral neck risk avascular necrosis because retinacular blood vessels are damaged.
Intracapsular fractures of the femoral neck have excellent blood supply and heal quickly.
Avascular necrosis only occurs in extracapsular fractures of the femur.
The law of ossification states that the epiphysis that appears first fuses last.
The law of ossification states that the epiphysis that appears first also fuses first.
Ossification centres appear simultaneously in all bones.
The lower end of the femur is the first secondary ossification centre to appear and is used to assess neonatal maturity.
The upper end of the femur is used to assess neonatal maturity at birth.
Ossification centres cannot be used to estimate gestational age.
